---
title: "Workflow"
bibliography: references.bib
---

## General Approach

This section outlines the workflow used for the assessments.

::: callout-tip
## Process

1.  Steps workflow diagram
:::

### Conceptual models

Overall the conceptual models in each bioregion helps to interpret and identify the ecological drivers of different stable states, to differentiate intact vegetation states from degraded states.

Palatability has been a key concept for decades used to inform degradation of natural vegetation in most biomes, especially in the Grassland and Nama Karoo. Continued selective grazing often leads to a transition from palatable perennial species to unpalatable and short-lived species. In some cases, this change may be detected with satellite imagery as colour or phenological shifts, if the altered species composition is markedly different.

Depending on the biome, different analytical steps are taken.

```{=html}
<!-- 
![Workflow Chart](imgs/workflow_chart.png)
-->
```
### Key pressures

The guidelines were used to prioritise the key pressures and degradation processes most relevant to assessing the ecological condition in each biome.

This section outlines the workflow used for the assessments.

::: callout-tip
## History of mapping degradation in South Africa

The utility of using remote sensing to map degradation was recognised as soon as the images became available in the 1970s. Several studies aimed to relate vegetation indices derived from satellite images, especially NDVI, to field-observed contrasts in rangeland condition. Analyses transitioned to time series as more satellite data were released and by the 2000s vegetation productivity trends became widely used proxies for degradation. Some recent mapping attempts have made significant progress toward mapping ecological condition. Most global products' resolution is inappropriate for relating it to ecological condition at the local scale needed for heterogenous ecosystems in South Africa. Though 1m products are now emerging, the accuracy remains low for South Africa, due to lack of ground truthing, which limits the use for land-use decision making. Therefore, we often still have to create our own condition data that is developed with the intention of conducting condition assessments from the outset.
:::

::: callout-tip
## The contentious nature of selecting a baseline condition

Ecologists rarely define baseline condition due to human influences being present for centuries, making it near impossible to observe a truly intact state of vegetation. In practice however, environmental impact assessment and restoration practitioners must have reference states to compare to. Similarly, to conduct spatial biodiversity assessments, a reference state is needed for relative coparison. Therefore, it is ideal to select the closest possible modern manifestation of an ecosystem type, based on consensus views. Conservation areas that have been protected for decades are often the only remnants left as intact examples of ecosystem types.
:::

###
